title: Marsdenia volubilis
taxonomy: Marsdenia volubilis is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. It is native to tropical and subtropical regions in the Americas, Australia, and parts of Asia. Marsdenia volubilis is classified as a vine or climber due to its ability to twine around other plants or structures. The plant's classification is further supported by its botanical name, which places it within the genus Marsdenia and the Apocynaceae family. The classification of Marsdenia volubilis is also reflected in its morphological characteristics, such as its ovate leaves and funnel-shaped flowers.

intro: Marsdenia volubilis, also known as the "Waxflower Vine," is a tropical plant species native to Southeast Asia, characterized by its evergreen, woody climber with fragrant, white flowers. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral activities, making it useful in treating various health conditions, such as fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Marsdenia volubilis include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as a decoction or tincture, and is also used in the production of various pharmaceutical formulations, including capsules and tablets.
